data.title: SLVR: Silver Miners Struggle To Find Their Footing
data.description:
Precious metals stocks just can’t seem to find their footing after the early-year surge. The Sprott Silver Miners & Physical Silver ETF (SLVR) is up 167% over the past 12 months, but it’s also down almost 30% from its January all-time high. I have a hold rating on the fund. While the valuation is decent, I am concerned about the technical situation. What’s more, the calendar is not exactly precious metal equities friend at the moment.

The upside is there, though. Central bank gold buying appears to be a secular tailwind for the whole space, while hopes for an improved global macroeconomic outlook could increase industrial demand for silver. Today, I’ll analyze SLVR’s fundamentals, the valuation, and technical clues.

SLVR Up Big YoY, But -30% From the January Intraday High

According to the issuer, SLVR is a pure-play fund designed to track the Nasdaq Sprott Silver Miners Index by investing at least 80% of its assets in silver-related securities. It provides unique exposure by combining investments in physical silver with shares in silver producers, developers, and explorers. The fund aims to capitalize on silver's role as both a precious metal with low asset correlation and a critical industrial material for high-growth sectors like solar energy and AI.

SLVR is a small ETF, with just $630 million in assets under management as of April 23, 2026. Its annual expense ratio is moderate to high at 65 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is high at 3.4%, which is more than two percentage points above that of the S&P 500. Of course, SLVR holds a high number of international stocks.

Share-price momentum is rated perfectly by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, but I’ll call out later that price action is by no means bullish on the multi-month view. As for risk, SLVR does not look ideal, which is quite common for precious metal funds. The historical realized standard deviation is high at 55%, and the ETF is highly concentrated in its largest holdings.

Liquidity is another issue. Average daily volume is decent at more than 500,000, but SLVR’s median 30-day bid/ask spread is wide at 41 basis points. Thus, I encourage prospective investors to use limit orders, particularly around the market open.

Looking closer at the portfolio, the product is focused in the lower-right section of the style box, indicating its bent to global small-cap growth stocks. There’s just 11% large-cap exposure, while only 4% of the fund is classified as value. Importantly, from a diversification perspective, almost 80% of SLVR is invested in shares of ex-US companies. That makes currency swings important to monitor (all else equal, a lower dollar is a boon to the portfolio).

On valuation, the ETF’s price-to-earnings ratio is not very high, but its long-term EPS growth rate is also muted. The result is a merely average PEG ratio.

SVLR: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

SLVR is almost entirely invested in the Materials sector when it's fully invested. Interestingly, there’s a more than 20% cash position. That has helped since the January top in silver and silver mining shares, but it would be a drag assuming price appreciation in its benchmark.

Moreover, with earnings season now in full swing, monitoring developments within its top position is key. First Majestic Silver (AG) is slated to report earnings next on May 12 in the premarket.

SLVR: Holdings & Dividend Information

For the seasonal checkup, I chose to analyze the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) due to its long track record. I conceded that GDX and SLVR do not mirror each other perfectly, but there’s a tight correlation.

With that in mind, we are entering a dicey calendar stretch, as returns from May through November have not been all that stellar since 2010.

GDX: Weaker Seasonal Period Ahead

The Technical Take

With a lackluster valuation and the potential for a rough several months ahead based on seasonality, SLVR’s technical situation is concerning. Notice in the chart below that the ETF now exhibits a bear flag pattern after notching its record high in January. The presumption here is that the trend of larger degree will unfold, meaning the next impulse move is down. Take a look at a comparable pattern during the uptrend—a bull flag from October and November 2025, which ultimately resolved higher.

The good news is that the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls still hold the primary trend. Resistance is obvious at the early-year highs (along with a lingering gap near $82). Support is at the March low and 200dma.

SLVR: Bear Flag In Play, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on SLVR. The technicals point to more downside risk than upside potential, while the valuation is not super compelling right now. Pay attention to AG’s earnings report next month, along with broader moves in silver and even gold.

data.title: Best Dividend Kings: April 2026
data.description:
2026 Review

Unlike the S&P 500 that continued sinking in the tail end of March, the Dividend Kings saw a little more positive momentum. It wasn't much, with the Kings collectively improving their March return from -6.93% as of the 19th to -5.78% at month end. The SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY) on the other hand declined further, falling from -3.83% on the 19th to -4.93% by month end. These losses may feel like a distant past given the strong rebound the stock market has seen in April thus far. The Dividend Kings are up 3.26% through April 24th, while SPY is up a whopping 8.94%.

Year-to-date the Kings are still fairing better than SPY, with a gain of 7.03% versus a gain of 4.18%.

Despite some sizable losses in March and a smaller rebound in April, 36 out of the 58 Dividend Kings continue to outperform SPY this year.

Best Dividend Kings in 2026

As of April 24th, 25 Dividend Kings have double-digit gains this year, with 6 up more than 20%. These are the 15 best-performing Kings:

  1. Gorman-Rupp Company (GRC) +56.20%
  2. Target (TGT) +34.49%
  3. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +31.00%
  4. H2O America (HTO) +26.22%
  5. Archer-Daniel-Midland (ADM) +22.74%
  6. Tootsie Roll (TR) +20.07%
  7. Walmart (WMT) +18.75%
  8. Nordson (NDSN) +18.42%
  9. Altria Group (MO) +18.39%
  10. Dover Corporation (DOV) +17.12%
  11. Northwest Natural Holding (NWN) +15.70%
  12. Grainger (GWW) +15.67%
  13. United Bancshares (UBSI) +15.45%
  14. Canadian Utilities (CDUAF) +14.20%
  15. Sonoco Products (SON) +13.79%

Only 13 Dividend Kings are sitting in the red year-to-date, down from 24 a month ago. The worst-performing Kings are Automatic Data Processing (ADP) -22.19%, Marzetti Company (MZTI) -23.30% and Abbott Labs (ABT) -25.82%.

Dividend Growth

Since my last update, 5 more Kings have announced a dividend increase. All were rather low and below prior years increases.

As of today, the average dividend growth rate for all of the Dividend Kings in 2026 stands at 3.11%, up from 2.98% a month ago.

Here are the recently announced dividend increases.

H.B. Fuller

Increased its dividend payout from $0.23 to $0.245, or by 4.26%.

Johnson & Johnson

Increased its dividend payout from $1.30 to $1.34, or by 3.08%.

Proctor and Gamble

Increased its dividend payout from $1.0568 to $1.0885, or by 3.00%.

Sonoco Products

Increased its dividend payout from $0.53 to $0.54, or by 1.89%.

Sysco

Increased its dividend payout from $0.54 to $0.55, or by 1.85%.

Earnings Projections

Since last month's update, analyst 3-5 year earnings growth projections have marginally trended higher by 0.09%, on average, for all the Dividend Kings.

12 Dividend Kings are currently projected to grow earnings by double digits over the next 3-5 years.

  1. AbbVie (ABBV) +21.32%
  2. ABM Industries (ABM) +10.25%
  3. Archer-Daniel-Midland (ADM) +15.62%
  4. Gorman-Rupp Company (GRC) +12.92%
  5. Grainger (GWW) +10.22%
  6. Middlesex Water Co. (MSEX) +11.82%
  7. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +24.46%
  8. Parker-Hannifin (PH) +11.04%
  9. Stepan Company (SCL) +28.24%
  10. S&P Global (SPGI) +12.18%
  11. Stanley Black & Decker (SWK) +14.96%
  12. Walmart (WMT) +12.05%

Promising Kings in 2026

In last month's update, as of March 18, I identified 29 Dividend Kings that appeared to be both potentially undervalued and had a strong long-term return estimate.

As of April 24th, 27 Dividend Kings appear to be both potentially undervalued and have a long-term annualized expected rate of return of at least 10%. Here they are:

Price Dividend Yield
Ticker Current Fair Over/Under Current Trailing Estimated ROR
ABM 40.51 58.96 -31.29% 2.86% 1.97% 20.91%
ABT 92.48 137.25 -32.62% 2.72% 1.84% 20.41%
ADM 70.03 74.09 -5.48% 2.97% 2.81% 19.72%
ADP 198.53 323.84 -38.70% 3.43% 2.10% 22.88%

PG 145.71 172.96 -15.75% 2.99% 2.52% 10.21%
PPG 110.29 140.77 -21.65% 2.58% 2.02% 14.95%
RPM 105.70 120.50 -12.28% 2.04% 1.79% 12.27%
SCL 51.77 84.15 -38.48% 3.05% 1.88% 41.50%
SON 49.19 58.40 -15.78% 4.39% 3.70% 15.44%
SPGI 439.03 463.38 -5.25% 0.88% 0.84% 14.15%
SWK 76.01 96.57 -21.29% 4.37% 3.44% 24.23%
SYY 75.81 84.74 -10.53% 2.90% 2.60% 11.57%
TGT 130.17 152.20 -14.48% 3.50% 3.00% 12.68%

Comparing this list to the prior month, 3 Kings have dropped off, and 1 new King were added.

Dropped:

  • Illinois Tool Works (ITW) - estimated return potential of 9.66%
  • Lowes Companies (LOW) - estimated return potential of 9.91%
  • Tennent Company (TNC) - estimated return potential of 7.51%

Added:

  • Sonoco Products (SON)

Dividend Yield Theory & Expected Rate of Return

I utilize Dividend Yield Theory as my primary valuation model for this analysis. Here is a brief explanation of how it works as well as my expected rate of return.

Dividend yield theory compares the forward dividend yield to the trailing 5-year average dividend yield to measure potential undervaluation. It is a backward looking valuation method based on reversion to the mean. It measures the average yield investors have been willing to pay for a given stock and assumes that over a 5-year rolling period on aggregate investors have established a fair value for the stock. It can be highly influenced by changes in dividend increase policies and short-term spikes and dips in share price.

The expected long-term annualized rate of return is measured as follows:

Expected ROR = 3-5 year analyst earnings growth forecast + forward dividend yield + 5-year annualized return for fair value based potential upside between the current price and the implied fair price.

Let's take a closer look at the dividend yield theory valuation for the 27 Dividend Kings on our "promising" list.

Please note that the data below may not precisely match the data in the table shown earlier, as the charts were pulled at a different time of the day and some share prices changed. Additionally, the stated dividend rate for 2026 is the forward annual dividend rate for each King; this may inherently overstate the 2026 dividend growth rate, but it is key to properly value each King.

ABM Industries (ABM)

Abbott Laboratories (ABT)

Please note that both the valuation provided by dividend yield theory and the expected rate of return are estimates based on several assumptions. These should not be interpreted as precise predictions of value and future return. Please conduct your own due diligence before making any investment decisions.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: S&P 500 Earnings: A Broken Record - Positive Revisions, Revenue Growth Worth Noting Again
data.description:
In last week’s post, this blog took a look at the S&P 500's expected revenue growth this year and then looked back at the last Trump Administration tax bill passed in December 2017 and found two things in common:

Tax Cuts & Jobs Act passed December ’17:

  • Q4 ’17 rev growth: +8.3%
  • Q1 ’28 rev growth: +8.4%
  • Q2 ’18 rev growth: +9.5%
  • Q3 ’18 rev growth: +8.6%
  • Q4 ’18 rev growth: +5.1%

This blog only started keeping revenue growth by quarter data in Q4 ’12, but the average revenue growth for all of that period – from Q4 ’12 through Q4 ’19 was just +3.2%.

OBBBA passed July ’25:

Here’s the “expected” revenue growth for 2026, by quarter, per the LSEG data:

  • Q4 ’26 rev growth: +8.6%
  • Q3 ’26 rev growth: +9.1%
  • Q2 ’26 rev growth: +10.3%
  • Q1 ’26 rev growth: +9.7%
  • Q4 ’25 actual rev growth: +9.2%
  • Q3 ’25 actual rev growth: +8.3%

The average rev growth rate per quarter from Q1 ’22 through Q4 ’25 (removing Covid’s distortions in 2020 and 2021) was +6.5%.

For 2025 and 2026, the difficulty for investors is separating how much is AI and “tech” and how much is tax-bill related. The difference was obvious in 2018, but not so much today.

The last 5 quarters' technology revenue growth per quarter:

  • Q1 ’26: +27% (estimated)
  • Q4 ’26: +21.5%
  • Q3 ’26: +16.8%
  • Q2 ’26: +16.3%
  • Q1 ’26: +13.3%

It’s easy to oversimplify, but layering the OBBBA onto the rest of the US economy on top of the continued white-hot AI buildout in terms of revenue growth and capex may be one reason why this market seems so strong today.

Just sayin…

S&P 500 data:

  • The forward 4-quarter S&P 500 EPS estimate increased again this week to $342.49, versus last week’s $340.96.
  • The PE on the forward estimate is 20.9x this week, versus 20.9x last week.
  • The S&P 500 earnings yield (S&P EY) ended the week at the same level as the prior week at 4.78%.
  • High-yield credit spreads have gradually tightened since early April ’26, from +342 to +284 (per Bespoke data).
  • Since January 2nd, ’26, the expected S&P 500 EPS growth has risen from 16% on 1/2/26 to 20% today (4/24/26).
  • The average “upside surprise” or beat rate for S&P 500 Q1 ’26 EPS reports is still 9.2%, while the average revenue upside surprise is 2.1%.

Here’s the weekly progression in S&P 500 weekly dollar estimates since January 2, ’26:

Quick summary

Within tech, hardware like semis and Dell (DELL) have been red hot, while software corrected again after IBM (IBM) and ServiceNow (NOW), so Wednesday night’s earnings lineup of Microsoft (MSFT), Meta (META), Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), and Amazon (AMZN) will be scrutinized closely.

It’s hard to think that if Microsoft is seriously dented by AI, the 2nd-tier software names can grow this part of the software business substantially. We’ll have to wait and see.

It’s another good earnings season again. Energy's expected 43.9% EPS growth rate for ’26 has now caught up to tech’s 44.3%.

Bet no one expected that to start 2026, and maybe more importantly, that the rest of the market would brush off the energy sector growth entirely, including bonds.

None of this is advice or a recommendation but only an opinion. Past performance is no guarantee of future results. None of this information may be updated, and if updated, may not be done so in a timely fashion. All EPS and revenue information is sourced from LSEG.

Thanks for reading.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: IAUI: Built For A Slower Gold Market
data.description:
I rate the NEOS Gold High Income ETF (IAUI) as a Buy. My thesis rests on three main points: gold has likely shifted out of its explosive rally phase and into a period of consolidation; the current implied volatility environment in gold options remains favorable for premium sellers; and IAUI's active, dynamic options strategy is perfectly suited to take advantage of current market conditions. IAUI is designed for exactly this kind of environment, and it performs best when gold stops rallying and starts moving sideways. IAUI, which won the "Best New Options Income ETF" from the 2026 ETF.com awards, offers a way to benefit from a sideways-moving gold market and collect double-digit yield paid out monthly while still having exposure to gold after its incredible rally.

Gold After The Rally

Any discussion of IAUI starts with its underlying gold. Gold had a massive run in 2025. Prices jumped as much as 55% and pushed past $4,000 per ounce. Moves like that don’t happen very often. They also expose the main weakness of covered call funds. When prices of the underlying surge quickly, the upside gets capped. While the option income is still there, IAUI has still performed very well. It looks small in comparison to the underlying stock appreciation. Looking ahead, we may see a different picture.

Ongoing geopolitical tension and softer economic growth support gold, but not in a way that drives another sharp rally. Instead, the more likely outcome is a sideways market, roughly between $4,500 and $5,500. That’s where IAUI starts to shine.

When prices move slowly or stay flat, option premiums become the main source of return. A standard gold ETF like the SPDR Gold Shares ETF (GLD) doesn’t offer that. A steady, slow uptrend would be ideal. IAUI still participates in gains while continuing to collect income along the way.

Covered call performance relies on volatility setup more than on price direction alone. It’s not enough for gold to move sideways. What matters is the gap between what options are pricing in and what actually plays out. After a strong rally, implied volatility in gold options often stays elevated. Macro uncertainty, rates, geopolitics, and all of that keep option premiums inflated even as the underlying price starts to cool off. Realized volatility, on the other hand, tends to settle down once the momentum fades. We can see from the CBOE Gold Volatility Index (^GVZ) that the long-run average for GVZ (since its inception in 2008) has generally hovered in the 16–18 range during calm periods. Readings in the 20s (what we have right now) are considered modestly elevated, the 30s reflect genuine stress, and anything in the 40s is historically associated with major market dislocations like COVID (2020), the 2008 financial crisis, or acute geopolitical shocks. Gold options are pricing in a lot of near-term uncertainty. That means premiums are expensive, and selling options looks attractive, and the high IV makes IAUI's dynamic options strategy shine. They can adapt to current market conditions and take full advantage of this volatility for both options income and price appreciation. It's a market that's being actively repriced, not just drifting higher on trend.

The Options Strategy

IAUI is an actively managed ETF with a dynamic options strategy. The structure is more capital-efficient: the fund holds roughly 63% in U.S. Treasury Bills, uses those as collateral to gain synthetic gold exposure through options, and holds about 24% in the Goldman Sachs Physical Gold ETF.

This structure has many benefits, because a large portion of the gold exposure is through options rather than physical gold holdings, and a big part of the cash position is freed to invest in short-term T-bills. That collateral income adds an extra return that is independent from gold entirely and even adds a layer of diversification into treasuries.

The fund managers at NEOS adjust how much of the portfolio is covered, where strikes are set, and how positions roll over time. Coverage usually sits between 50% and 100%, not a full overwrite every single options cycle. It means the fund still leaves room for stock price appreciation.

Looking deeper into the prospectus, we can see that the return is mainly driven by three components. Treasury income from collateral, options premium income, and residual gold exposure. This matters because it separates IAUI from traditional gold ETFs that just track the price of the underlying. Traditional gold ETFs rely entirely on price appreciation. In a flat market, the exposure to T-bills and selling options will carry total returns even if gold price appreciation does not contribute much.

A large portion of IAUI’s distributions has been classified as return of capital. Instead of paying taxes immediately, investors lower their cost basis and deal with taxes later when they sell. For taxable accounts, that’s a real advantage. Referring to my article on the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), where NEOS employs the same tax efficiency strategy for IAUI, we can see that:

The IRS has specific types of "contracts" that get special tax treatment. One of these contracts is index options, as the ones we use on QQQI. These are called Section 1256 contracts. These contracts allow our profits to be taxed 60% as long-term capital gains, and 40% as short-term capital gains. This is a major win as long-term capital gains are taxed at a way lower rate than short-term, and these rules apply even if you held the contract for 1 day.

Additionally, much of the payout capital returned so far has been labeled as return on capital (ROC), which is not taxed immediately, and instead lowers your cost basis and is only taxed when the shares are sold or your cost basis becomes 0.

So far 87%-90% of IAUI's distributions have been ROC. Note also that once the cost basis reaches zero, gains above that are taxed as long-term capital gains.

Total Return Vs. Peers

The closest comparison is the UBS ETRACS Gold Shares Covered Call ETN (GLDI), which has been around much longer. GLDI's inception was in 2013, while IAUI's was just in June of 2025. GLDI runs a fully fixed mechanical strategy. It sells calls on 100% of its exposure every time. That produces consistent income, especially when gold isn’t moving much. IAUI takes a different approach. With partial coverage and active management, it leans more toward total return, factoring in price appreciation. The difference becomes especially noticeable when looking at implied vs. realized volatility environments. In a high-IV environment, something we would find in a macro-driven rally, GLDI's options strategy fully locks in premiums and caps the upside. While IAUI can selectively reduce the coverage and adjust the strikes further and higher, which allows them to participate in the rally.

In a low volatility environment, both of the strategies will struggle to generate a premium, but IAUI benefits from the treasury income. GLDI being fully dependent on options income becomes more sensitive to declining premiums, hindering total returns.

In the 2025 rally, IAUI outperformed by a noticeable margin. As the gold price consolidated, that gap shrank. In my personal opinion, I prefer dynamic options strategies to mechanical ones, and the results do speak for themselves.

This references a 6-month time frame due to IAUI's recent inception.

Since launch, IAUI has trailed a plain gold ETF on total returns. Although not by much, in the last 6 months IAUI has returned 12.75%, while GLD has returned 13.79%. This is in a gold rally, the worst possible scenario for a covered call ETF. If the market has any sort of pullback or remains stagnant for a while, we will see IAUI outperform the underlying. The key factor to take into account is not whether IAUI will underperform GLD in acute rare gold rallies, but if IAUI will outperform or match performance in a risk-adjusted, lower-volatility, and lower-beta way in a full cycle. If gold enters a range-bound stagnant period or trends downwards, IAUI will outperform. It's important to also note that there is no hard floor created by long puts for IAUI, and the income offsets drawdowns in terms of total return. The price and NAV would also drop during gold's downturn.

We can see this clearly with Bitcoin the NEOS High Income Bitcoin ETF (BTCI) vs. the Grayscale Bitcoin Mini Trust ETF (BTC). BTCI is another NEOS fund with a similar options strategy as IAUI. While both BTCI and BTC have not performed very well in this crypto bear cycle we are experiencing, we see here that BTCI in total returns is taking the lead. If gold after this monster rally has a period of stagnation or a bigger pullback like bitcoin, we could see IAUI outperform.

The Risk That Matters Most

The biggest long-term risk to any income ETF is NAV erosion over time. IAUI’s yield sits around the low teens, which is expected from an ETF using gold as the underlying. There are many more volatile assets than gold. Funds pushing 20% or more face far greater pressure from NAV erosion.

If gold stays range-bound and volatility remains healthy, the option income should support distributions. If gold enters a prolonged decline, both NAV and payouts come under pressure. This isn’t a hedged product. It’s still a long gold position with an income layer on top.

Why The Buy Rating

The case for IAUI comes down to timing and structure. With a 12.52% distribution yield and a 26.26% cumulative return since inception, all backed by a relatively low-risk underlying asset like gold, the risk-reward profile stands out in the current environment. Gold looks more likely to move sideways than explode higher; this plays directly into IAUI’s strengths. Volatility remains high enough to keep premiums attractive. That supports a steady income stream, and the fund offers a different way to hold gold, one that combines exposure, income, and some downside cushioning.

More importantly, IAUI isn’t built only for flat markets. It pulls returns from three different sources: option premiums, collateral income, and some upside when gold moves higher. That mix gives it a steadier return profile. It’s less one-dimensional than GLD, which relies on price alone, and more flexible than fully overwritten peers like GLDI that give up most of their upside.

Bottom Line

In a unique period of higher gold volatility, IAUI fits a specific type of investor, an investor looking for gold exposure while also looking to collect monthly income. If you expect gold to move sideways or climb slowly, and you want a steady income source along the way, it makes sense.

data.title: Mispriced CVR Partners Looks Ready To Grow
data.description:
In 2025, nitrogen fertilizer company CVR Partners' (UAN) net income nearly doubled to $99 million, spurred on by revenues of $606 million. Although impressive, perhaps the most important development for the company is the Iran War and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. This has caused a nitrogen fertilizer supply chain disruption that is unprecedented in history. CVR will benefit from the substantial pricing opportunities that this supply chain disruption will cause because of its distance from the turmoil in the Persian Gulf, while competitors in the Middle East, South Asia, and Europe struggle with stranded production and logistics paralysis. For these reasons, CVR Partners is a strong buy.

Company Overview & Geographic Markets

CVR Partners is a Delaware master limited partnership that operates two nitrogen fertilizer plants in the U.S. Corn Belt. One plant is in Coffeyville, Kansas, and another is in East Dubuque, Illinois. The partnership produces anhydrous ammonia and urea ammonia nitrate (“UAN”) solution, the dominant liquid nitrogen fertilizer used on U.S. row crops. Whereas most global nitrogen producers rely on imported natural gas, CVR Partners sources feedstock from the U.S. domestic gas market at Henry Hub-linked prices. These prices have historically traded at a significant discount to European and Asian benchmarks.

In addition, CVR Partners enjoys a cost advantage from its Coffeyville facility, which ingests cheap petroleum coke from CVR Energy. No other facility in the country takes petroleum coke as an input. In periods such as this, when natural gas prices are at relatively high prices, petroleum costs, which are cheaper, give the Coffeyville facility an advantage. Management has touted this cost advantage repeatedly.

The partnership sells to the agricultural heartland of the United States, to Kansas, Missouri, Nebraska, Iowa, Illinois, Colorado, and Texas. Its proximity to some of the most productive and nitrogen-intensive farmland in the world. CVR plants are close to markets, which averts logistical challenges and supply chain disruptions. Moreover, proximity supports higher realized prices compared to coastal import terminals and insulates the partnership from the maritime supply chain disruptions such as the ones currently reshaping global fertilizer markets. This is important for CVR since fertilizer product sales of $432 million in 2025 accounted for 71.3% of net sales.

On the Q4 2025 earnings call (February 19, 2026), CEO Mark Pytosh reiterated that Europe faces long-term natural gas supply issues, which will likely persist through 2026. Such a scenario creates export opportunities for ammonia and acknowledges tighter global supply. Notably, his remarks came before the Iran War. European gas has exceeded $13/MMBtu since the start of 2026. In comparison, U.S. natural gas remains at a significant discount to international benchmarks, trading at $3.05 at the time of writing. CVR Partners sells into a high-price environment while maintaining relatively contained input costs.

The Iran Conflict: A Once-In-A-Generation Supply Shock

The single largest catalyst for the CVR Partners investment case in 2026 has been the disruption caused by Operation Epic Fury/Roaring Lion and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. A report leaked from the Pentagon said it would take six months to clear the strait of mines. The Pentagon has rejected those claims, but the issue of mines persists. In addition, from a base rate point of view, major treaties take years to negotiate, and in that time, disagreements could lead to a re-closing, even if the strait was reopened. The Obama Administration's Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action (JCPOA) was negotiated over 20 months of talks. I estimate that from START I to the JCPOA, the average bilateral/multilateral nuclear and arms-control negotiations have taken two to three years.

The strategic importance of this waterway cannot be overstated. The American Farm Bureau Federation said that disruptions in the Persian Gulf “account for nearly 49% of global urea exports and approximately 30% of global ammonia exports.” Major exporters such as Iran, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates (UAE), and Kuwait have seen their shipment routes effectively cut off, stranding millions of tons of product.

The price response has been swift and severe. According to Pro Farmer data as of April 10, 2026, in the six weeks since hostilities commenced, NOLA UAN prices surged approximately 38% ($145/ton), anhydrous ammonia at the Midwest level climbed 32% ($245/ton), and NOLA urea jumped 49% ($230/ton). Analysts told CNBC that Egyptian FOB urea, which is a global benchmark, jumped from around $400–490 per ton to approximately $700 per metric ton; it has since risen to a high of $830 per ton. Reflecting the gravity of the crisis and uncertainties over the conflict’s duration, Fitch Ratings raised its 2026 ammonia and urea price expectations by around 25%.

On the Q4 2025 earnings call, Mr. Mark Pytosh flagged 'higher than historical volatility' for 2026 and specifically called out geopolitical risk from the Middle East, North Africa, and Russian capacity as a multi-quarter tail risk, not a transient event.

Financial Performance: A Recovering Earnings Base

The Persian Gulf might be a critical part of the 2026 story, but recent financial releases remain instructive of the partnership’s condition. Full-year 2025 represented a meaningful recovery from the 2024 trough, although results continue to hover below the 2022–2023 peak cycle. Net sales rose 15.4% year-over-year while EBITDA expanded and net income nearly doubled to $99 million.

Metric

FY2023

FY2024

FY2025

Net Sales ($M)

$606

Net Income ($M)

$99

EBITDA ($M)

$281

$211

EPS (per unit)

$16.31

$5.76

$9.33

Sources: SEC filings—Q4/FY2025 8-K; Q4/FY2024 8-K

The pricing improvement across both major products was the primary driver. For full-year 2025, the average gate prices for ammonia rose 22% to $582/ton, and UAN improved 27% to $314/ton.

However, Q4 2025 was an anomaly as the partnership recorded a net loss of $10 million driven by unplanned maintenance at the Coffeyville plant. This increased maintenance capex pushed ammonia utilization down to 64%. The $0.37 per unit distribution declared for Q4 reflected this temporary disruption. Importantly, management projected a return to 95–100% utilization in Q1 2026 and guided direct operating expenses of $57–62 million for the quarter, signaling a clean normalization. However, they have also indicated that substantial capex will be utilized in increasing the capacity of one ammonium plant and purification of water. This is significant given that total debt and finance lease obligations as of year-end stood at $569.8 million, with total liquidity of $117 million, including $69 million in cash.

The 2025 cumulative distribution of $11.92 per common unit highlights the income-generative capacity of the model in a supportive pricing environment.

Valuation: Pricing In Pessimism

CVR Partners units trade at valuations that betray the extraordinary near-term pricing catalyst. At approximately $122.99 per unit, 13.18 P/E, which is a fraction of the broader U.S. Chemicals industry average of 20.55, the sector median of 23.83, and the peer average of 18.49. I computed the following target price by averaging the sector median, peer, and industry averages to get an equally weighted target price of $195.53, with an upside of 50.13%.

P/E Multiple Source

P/E

Target Price = EPS × P/E

Upside/(Downside)

Sector Median

23.83

$ 222.33

70.71%

Peers

18.49

$ 172.51

32.46%

Damodaran

20.55

$ 191.73

47.21%

Equally Weighted Target

20.96

$ 195.53

50.13%

Sources: Seeking Alpha, Damodaran, and other calculations

At current levels, the stock appears deeply undervalued relative to its peers and sector medians and industry averages. Against each benchmark, CVR Partners, the results are positive.

Overall, while the exact level of upside varies, all benchmarks point in the same direction. The stock appears attractively valued, with a favorable risk-reward profile, provided earnings remain steady. But what accounts for the undervaluation? My reading is that CVR Partners’ stock is undervalued due to its Master Limited Partnership Structure, which limits institutional ownership and creates tax complexities, suppressing demand. This discount is likely to shift as the market recognizes the company’s true value.

Key Risks

CVR Partners is not without meaningful risks. Commodity price cyclicality is the main concern since nitrogen fertilizer prices are volatile and mean-reverting. The 2023 peak ($681M revenue, $16.31 EPS) was followed by a severe correction in 2024. If the Iran conflict de-escalates rapidly and Hormuz shipping resumes as has been hinted in negotiations between the warring parties, the supply shock premium could dissipate quickly.

Operational concentration is a secondary concern. With two plants, any significant unplanned outage (as occurred at Coffeyville in Q4 2025) can materially impair quarterly results and distributions. Already, the partnership has announced its capital spending plan for 2026, amounting to between $60M and $76M. The investments preserve capacity and enhance margins. They support sustainability, ensuring better operational performance in the short to medium term.

Natural gas cost exposure is structural. While U.S. Henry Hub prices currently sit well below international benchmarks, domestic gas is not immune. Management noted U.S. gas trending $3–4/MMBtu in Q4 2025 but flagged LNG export capacity growth as a factor that could narrow the global price differential over time.

Finally, although it certainly has benefits for some, the Master Limited Partnership tax structure creates complexity for some investor categories. This potentially suppresses institutional demand, contributing to the persistent valuation discount relative to C-corporation peers.

Conclusion: Bullish with Conviction

CVR Partners does not ask investors to buy into the narrative and take a leap of faith. It asks them to read a geographic, logistical, and supply chain map. The US-Israel-Iran war looms large over the fertilizer market, bearing significance even for CVR Partners, whose location and markets are distant from the Persian Gulf. With supply constraints, prices have spiked, and this has positive implications for the partnership, promising impeccable returns. Complementing the effects of war is a valuation framework that suggests immense room for growth. The valuation framework I employed points to a consistent conclusion: the stock is priced at a level that does not fully reflect its normalized earnings potential.

Conservatism appears rooted in the market’s treatment of current fertilizer pricing, particularly concerning NOLA urea benchmarks. By anchoring valuation in these conditions, the market risks underestimating the cyclical nature of earnings in the sector. History suggests that pricing environments can shift meaningfully, with even modest recovery translating into disproportionate gains in profitability.

In that context, the investment case rests less on near-term conditions and more on cycle normalization. If pricing stabilizes or improves, the gap between current valuation and intrinsic value could narrow significantly, supporting a re-rating.

data.title: Ball Corporation Isn't Prepared For An Upgrade Yet
data.description:
Over a year ago now, in the middle of February of 2025, I wrote my last article about Ball Corporation (BALL). Conceptually, I find it to be an interesting business. It plays in the global beverage packaging space through the production of aluminum cans and other products. I know that to many investors, that might not be exciting. But to me, it certainly is. But in that article, I made the decision to reaffirm the company as a ‘hold’ candidate. This was in spite of the fact that, the previous year, the company brought in $4.5 billion in after-tax proceeds from selling off its aerospace division. The fact of the matter was that, at the time, the stock was priced at levels that looked pretty much fairly valued to me. But since then, shares have risen 33.1%, comfortably outpacing the 16.5% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw over the same window of time.

I will admit that I was perhaps a bit too cautious. However, looking at the picture today, I still feel very similarly to how I felt back then. On an absolute basis, shares are not exactly expensive. But they certainly aren't in the undervalued category either. In fact, relative to other similar firms, the stock is a bit pricey. And even with growth expectations for this year, it does not dip into the undervalued category. Because of this, I have no choice but to reaffirm the business as a ‘hold’ candidate. But as new data comes in, that could change. Fortunately, the next data is expected to come out before the market opens on May 5th. Leading up to that point, analysts anticipate a rise in revenue and profits. But unless management ends up significantly revising higher expectations for this year, I think I will stick with my neutral assessment of it.

I Can't Pop The Top On This Play

At its core, Ball Corporation is, to me, a very interesting business. It has a long history, dating back to 1880. And from those origins in the state of Indiana, the company has expanded into a multinational player in the packaging industry. Specifically, the company focuses on producing and selling aluminum beverage containers, though it does produce other things as well, such as extruded aluminum aerosol containers, re-closeable aluminum bottles, and aluminum slugs.

The business achieves this through a significant network of assets that includes 17 manufacturing facilities in the U.S., one in Canada, and one in Mexico. Overwhelmingly, the business is focused on North and Central America, with those regions accounting for about 48% of revenue last year. This is, truly, a massive market. Management mentioned that in North America alone, aluminum beverage containers amounted to 139 billion units last year. Management did not provide unit estimates for Central America. But the firm did say that, between those markets, it sold 50 billion aluminum beverage containers last year. This is not all that the company has. It also boasts 19 facilities spread throughout Europe, as well as one in Turkey and one in Egypt. It produces about 39% of the 97 billion containers produced annually in those markets. And then in South America, it has 12 facilities and is responsible for 46% of the 43 billion containers that are produced every year in that region.

Over the last three years now, financial performance for the business has been admittedly mixed. Revenue dropped from 2023 to 2024 before jumping in 2025. The rise from $11.80 billion in revenue in 2024 to $13.16 billion in 2025 was thanks to a combination of factors. Management mentioned, for instance, that as aluminum prices (LMAHDS03:COM) moved higher, the company was able to push those costs onto customers. That accounted for $579 million of the increase in revenue that the business saw during that year. Even larger was a rise in volume that benefited the business to the tune of $713 million. Plus, it saw a $177 million benefit from foreign currency fluctuations.

Thanks to the higher volume and higher prices, profitability for the company improved also. Net income of $912 million in 2025 comfortably exceeded the $424 million reported the previous year. Operating cash flow skyrocketed from $115 million to $1.26 billion. And if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $666 million to $1.39 billion. Finally, EBITDA for the company expanded from $1.94 billion to $2.04 billion.

When it comes to the 2026 fiscal year, management seems quite optimistic. They expect earnings per share to rise by 10% or more. We know that, over the last three years now, management bought back about 16% of all shares that the business had outstanding. So we don't know exactly what kind of impact further share buybacks will have on the business. But if we project out results based on a static share price count, we would be looking at net profits for this year of about $962 million. If we use management’s other guidance figures, we would get adjusted operating cash flow of $1.50 billion and EBITDA of $2.12 billion.

Some of this benefit is certainly coming from changes that management is making to its portfolio. In January of this year, for instance, the company acquired an 80% capital share of the European beverage can manufacturing business of Benepack from ORG Technology Co. This cost the company $218 million, with $95 million of that in the form of cash and the rest in the form of the assumption of additional debt. And last February, the company acquired Florida Can Manufacturing, which operates as an aluminum beverage can manufacturing firm, in a transaction worth $160 million. Of course, the company does not focus only on buying up assets. Management is not afraid to sell them off when it makes sense to as well. Last August, for instance, the company sold 41% of its 51% ownership in Ball United Arab Can Manufacturing Company in a deal valued at $71 million. This was its operation in Saudi Arabia.

Keeping in mind that the picture for the business can change as additional acquisitions and potentially divestitures are made, in the chart above, you can see how the company is valued using estimates for 2026 and historical results for 2025. These aren't bad multiples, but they certainly don't make the company look cheap either. In fact, in that chart, you can see how the business stacks up against five other similar firms. Three of them are cheaper than it is on a price-to-earnings basis, while another one is tied with it. Three ended up being cheaper on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, I found that four of these companies were cheaper than it.

Even though I have decided to maintain my neutral stance on the firm, that picture could change as new data justifies it. In the table below, you can see that analysts believe that revenue for the first quarter of the 2026 fiscal year, the data of which should come out before the market opens on May 5th, will be around $3.34 billion. That would be up nicely from the $3.10 billion that the business reported a year earlier. With the rise in revenue should come higher profits. Analysts expect, for instance, for earnings per share to climb from $0.64 to $0.77. That would translate to a rise in net income from $181 million to $205 million. In all likelihood, that would translate to higher cash flows as well, and investors would be wise to keep an eye out on those figures when they are announced.

Takeaway

As much as I would love to take a bullish stance on Ball Corporation right now, I just can't pull the trigger on an upgrade. As a value investor, I look for the best opportunities. And while I don't necessarily think that this candidate will be bad, I just don't see it continuing to outperform the market like it has. In light of this, I have no choice but to maintain BALL stock as a Hold for now.

data.title: GoPro: Reiterating My Sell Rating On Weak Demand And Poor Fundamentals
data.description:
I have been a long-standing bear on GoPro's (GPRO) business, starting my coverage back in 2022 with an initial sell rating, which I maintained all the way up until my latest writing. While the stock price is down significantly, I still do not believe that GoPro is an attractive business to own. The lack of demand, the macroeconomic headwinds, and key balance sheet line items are still preventing me from assigning the stock a more favorable rating. The aim of my writing today is to highlight the most relevant factors and why I believe that my bearish view is still warranted.

1. Revenue and demand

In the most recent quarter, GPRO reported revenue that was roughly in-line with the results delivered in the same quarter in the prior year, but if we look at fiscal 2025, revenue is down by 19%. At the same time, the gross margins have contracted, but due to the lower operating expenses, the firm actually managed to achieve a positive adjusted EBITDA. The firm still generated a net loss, however.

Let us break down the revenue a bit and highlight what I exactly find unattractive. First, in fiscal 2025, the number of camera units shipped fell by 25%. This is a clear indication that people are not keen on getting GoPro's products anymore. On one hand, it may be a sign that the firm is not innovative enough; on the other hand - and I believe this is a bigger reason - the competition in the past years intensified meaningfully. By this I mean that GoPro is not only having to compete with other action camera manufacturers but also with smartphone manufacturers, like Apple (AAPL), or firms active in the smart glass space, like Meta (META). Second, with average unit price going up and units shipped falling, it's also a strong indication that the demand for the products is very elastic - meaning that the higher the price, the less the people will buy.

Subscription revenue stayed flat, which I also find disappointing, because this could have been an interesting growth segment of the firm, but seems like it's not. (In my previous writing, I actually highlighted subscription and the opt-in AI program as potential growth drivers.) At least not yet. But considering that subscriber count actually fell by 7% in Q4 year-over-year, it may be an indication that the core community that supports GoPro may be shrinking. It's also important to point out that revenue fell through both channels - retail and GoPro.com. The revenue decrease was also relatively widespread, revenue falling in all regions, except the Americas.

While I mentioned that the lack of demand may be related to the lack of innovation or the intensifying competition, we also cannot forget about the challenging macroeconomic landscape - which is not only painful to GoPro but to many firms active in the consumer discretionary space. I often use consumer confidence to try to gauge, how demand for discretionary goods may develop. The reason I like this indicator is because it's forward-looking. If the confidence is low right now, it means that people are not likely to spend large sums on non-essential, discretionary products until their financial situation or outlook meaningfully improves - because they're concerned that hard times may be ahead. And such improvements do not happen from one day to another. They're gradual, they take time, and they need to be consistent so that people feel stability ahead.

To sum up this section, my main takeaway from the results is that despite all the efforts, GoPro is still not able to reignite customer excitement and demand for both their products and services. I cannot get less bearish on a firm, which is struggling to effectively differentiate its products from the competition or at least provide a clear outlook on how it plans to regain market share and market relevance.

Upside potential

GoPro partnered with helmet manufacturer AGV, which could potentially increase the addressable market and boost demand. While it may seem like a good opportunity, GoPro already had a try at entering the space by acquiring a helmet manufacturer, Forcite. It did not seem to help, however.

2. Expenses

When looking at expenses, one could think that GoPro is doing better now. On one hand, I find it attractive that the firm is having much better cost control, but the fact that they're still generating a net loss makes it difficult to get too excited about. It needs to be highlighted, however, that gross margin declined by 20 bps due to higher product costs and higher tariffs. Research and development costs decreased significantly due to lower spending on consulting and professional services, decreased cash-based personnel-related costs, and lower stock-based compensation. SG&A expenses also declined, with the largest driver being the lower advertising and marketing expenses. Also important to note that the firm cut its workforce by 23% recently.

While I believe effective cost control is necessary, and GoPro is doing the right thing, it may not be enough to turn the company around.

At this point, I have to bring up the macro landscape once again, as GoPro is not immune from commodity price fluctuations either. What I would like to highlight today is the oil price - as it's driving both manufacturing and transportation costs. When the war in Iran broke out, and the Strait of Hormuz was closed, oil prices skyrocketed, as many countries from the Middle East were (and are still) not able to transport oil through the strait to the market. As long as the reopening of the strait is unclear, I expect oil prices to remain high, negatively impacting not only GoPro's profits, but the global economy overall. Some firms that have a moat, a differentiated product portfolio, or a more loyal customer base may be more successful at withstanding the commodity price headwinds, as it may be easier for them to pass on costs to their customers. But in my view, GoPro does not belong to this group, as the demand for their products is already weak.

All in all, I find it good that GoPro is cutting costs, as it helps lower losses, but without reigniting the demand and increasing sales, I do not believe that the firm will be able to strive.

3. Balance sheet

When it comes to the balance sheet, I would like to discuss three items: Accounts receivable, current assets, and current liabilities.

Accounts receivable grew roughly 10% year-over-year. If accounts receivable grow at a faster pace than revenue, it can be a warning sign that the firm is selling more on credit - potentially pulling demand forward from future periods - or changed its revenue recognition. With revenue declining and accounts receivable growing, this is definitely something that investors need to be careful about.

When it comes to financial flexibility, liquidity ratios are a good place to start. If current assets are enough to cover current liabilities, it's normally an attractive sign. If it can be done by excluding the inventories - even better. In GoPro's case, both the current- and quick ratios are below one, meaning that current assets are not sufficient to cover current liabilities. This limits the financial flexibility of the firm as well.

For this reason, I find GoPro's balance sheet unattractive.

4. Valuation

Considering the fundamentals, I do not believe that there's much to discuss about the valuation. Even if some of the price multiples would indicate that GPRO is attractively valued, it does not mean much. With declining sales, with declining camera units sold, and with declining subscriber count, there's not much reason to get excited about any valuation metrics.

For this reason, I still believe that GoPro is a value trap at best.

Conclusions

GoPro remains unattractive fundamentally. Sales have significantly declined in fiscal 2025; the number of camera units sold fell, and subscriber count in Q4 also declined by 7%.

The firm is putting significant effort into effectively controlling costs, and it seems to be working. Operating expenses were significantly down in both Q4 and fiscal 2025. In my view, this alone is not going to turn the business around, however.

The firm's balance sheet remains unattractive with growing accounts receivable and with liquidity ratios below 1.

For these reasons, I reiterate my sell rating

data.title: Travelzoo: Improved Earnings On Seasonally Cheaper CAC, But Its Economics Don't Seem Attractive
data.description:
Travelzoo (TZOO) recently reported 1Q26 earnings.

The stock jumped significantly after the release, I would believe primarily on the sequential improvement in operating income. This change in results probably stems from lower advertising expenses, which, according to the company, are a source of profits down the road.

The results do not solve a concern expressed in previous quarters, however, about whether the economics of the new membership model, with high CACs, work at all, despite already being more than a year into the program.

With the additional risks to international travel posed by the conflict in the Middle East, I believe the stock is unattractive at these prices, which are higher than when I last rated a stock a Hold. Therefore, I maintain a Hold rating on TZOO.

1Q26 Results

The main data point of the earnings release is the company posting operating income of $3.4 million for the quarter. This was below the profits last year ($3.8 million, $4.4 million on a non-GAAP basis), despite higher revenues, implying lower margins, but they were a significant improvement on a sequential basis.

The main difference with previous quarters, like 4Q25, is a decrease in G&A (positive because G&A is not a value-generating activity for the customer) and lower relative S&M.

The lower relative S&M might be explained by the lower CAC. During the call, the company said that "average acquisition cost per club member was $28 in Q1 2025, $38 in Q2, $40 in Q3, $34 in Q4, and $27 in Q1 2026." This is probably the result of seasonality, as targeting travel-oriented customers online is probably cheaper during the travel offseason in Q4 and Q1 and more expensive during spring-summer (Q2 and Q3).

Revenues continued to grow, but the question of the higher revenues is whether they are profitable. Advertising revenues stemming from the legacy business were flat YoY. The revenues that grew came from memberships.

As done in previous quarters, we can estimate the net gain in members by assuming that the net increase in membership revenues ($500 thousand) is divided across $10 or $12.5 (the company recently raised prices from $40 to $50 per year in the US) of the first quarter accrual recognition of a new member. This indicates about 40 or 50 thousand net new members. The change in deferred revenues of $1.2 million also points to 40 thousand new members if we assume that the deferred figures are $30 per net new user.

The problem is that if the company wanted to acquire 40 or 50 thousand new members in Q2 or Q3, it would need to pay 40% to 50% more than in Q1 (based on the above disclosure). Given that it spent $2 million in Q1 (earnings presentation page 7), it will need to spend about $3 million in Q2 and Q3. This will reduce the profitability of the company back to 2Q25 levels (around $2 million).

Furthermore, the $2 million figure for aggregate customer acquisition costs is interesting. The company spent that figure and gained between 40 and 50 thousand net new members, who paid each $40 to $50. That is, the company spent $2 million (without considering overhead or others) to generate $1.6 to $2.5 million in future revenues. Again, the economics are not as good as the company shows with the CAC figure alone.

Furthermore, if the company spent $2 million at a CAC of $27, then it actually registered 74 thousand members but lost between 24 and 34 thousand to reach a net 40 to 50 thousand. The churned members have to have joined in 1Q25. At the time, the company had added 80 thousand net new members ($800 thousand difference in membership revenues between 1Q25 and 4Q24, as shown on page 6 of the earnings presentation 1Q25). This could imply a churn rate of as much as 40% per year, although it is potentially lower because the gross added members are probably more than 80 thousand (we cannot know because the company did not report aggregate acquisition costs for this business).

At, say, 30% churn per year, to keep the customer file constant at 460 thousand (that is, recover 138 thousand members per year), at an average CAC per customer of $35 (average of the four quarters mentioned above), the company would need to spend almost $5 million per year in direct marketing costs, or close to 30% of the revenues generated by that same user base ($18.4 million per year at $40).

Assuming that the members generate a margin above marketing costs of 24%, just like the rest of the operation, they are likely only barely profitable or even unprofitable. The 24% results from removing the $2 million in direct marketing from the operating expenses + CoGS in Q1 ($20.7 million, that is $18.7 million) and dividing by 1Q26 revenues of $24.5 million to reach CoGS+OpEx/Revenues of ~76%, or a contribution of 24%.

Valuation

Today, the company trades at a market cap of $104 million.

Assuming that the economics I presented above are accurate (and I should warn that they are highly speculative but the best we can do with the data at hand), at a constant run rate (that is, without spending on adding new customers), the company might be able to generate $97 million in revenues, which, using a contribution of 24% before marketing expenses for the membership business, implies $23 million. From this, we have to remove $5 million in expected marketing costs to keep the membership business constant, or $17 million. After taxes, this becomes $12 million.

This might seem attractive, but it is based on relatively optimistic economics, like a relatively low churn assumption or consistent CAC costs that do not grow over time.

In terms of the quality of the underlying business, the fact that the advertising has been working for several years, even decades, is a sign that some find it valuable. Still, it is a business that has not been growing in years.

I have doubts about whether memberships will be as durable and profitable, which are part of my discussion about long-term economics, particularly regarding churn levels.

Finally, on the qualitative front, we should remember this is a travel business that is exposed to discretionary income cycles. We should also add the quite relevant risk of disruption in air travel and tourism caused by already close to 2 months of a blockade in the Persian Gulf, which is affecting jet fuel prices and jet fuel supplies, on top of the myriad impacts on discretionary income that a general supply shock will have on the global economy.

In my opinion, TZOO's current share price could be considered a fair valuation if one used the above assumptions around business economics. However, given the lack of consistency in improving economics and the challenges presented to global tourism activity in 2026, the name is not attractive at current prices. I maintain a Hold rating.

data.title: Federal Reserve Watch: Inflation Coming?
data.description:
The Federal Reserve added $9.9 billion in securities to its securities in the past banking week.

This was the twenty-first banking week since the Fed decided to begin another round of quantitative easing.

In this twenty-one-week period, the Federal Reserve has added $168.7 billion to its portfolio of securities held outright. This is an average of $8.0 billion per week over this period of time.

In the eight weeks since the "war" in Iran began, the Federal Reserve has added $81.5 billion to its securities portfolio. This is an average of just over $10.0 billion per week over this period of time.

The Federal Reserve has added securities to its securities portfolio at a faster pace since the "war" began, but the increase per week has not seemed to be that much greater.

Conclusion: The Federal Reserve has added securities to its portfolio at a faster rate since the start of the "war" with Iran, but the weekly addition has not been that large. The "war" has not changed things that much.

Here is a picture of how the portfolio has risen during this time period.

The important thing is that President Trump has wanted the Federal Reserve to lower its policy rate of interest.

Adding securities to the securities portfolio does increase the reserves of the banking system.

However, while the Federal Reserve has been increasing the size of its securities portfolio, it has also been doing other things within its balance sheet to offset this increase.

As a consequence, the Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks have not changed much at all.

The Reserve Balances with Federal Reserve banks can be thought of as the "excess reserves" of the banking system.

Over the past twenty-one weeks, the "excess reserves" in the banking system have risen by $16.6 billion, which is almost nothing. The "total" Reserve Balances in the commercial banking system were $2.9 trillion on April 22, 2026.

It does not appear as if the commercial banking system has seen any real growth in "excess reserves" over this time period.

One could argue that as the "excess reserves" of the banking system rose...there would be pressure on the Fed's policy rate of interest...the Federal Funds rate...to fall.

The Federal Reserve seems to be managing the "excess reserves" of the banking system in such a way that there is little "market pressure" for the Federal Funds rate to fall.

Consequently, little has been done in this area since the start of December 2026. Only one rate reduction has taken place.

So, over the past 21 banking weeks (Wednesday to Wednesday), the Federal Reserve has moved to a policy of quantitative easing, but they have managed their balance sheet in such a way that Fed officials have only felt the need to lower the Fed's policy rate of interest one time during this period.

But the commercial banking system is "loaded" with reserves!!!

And the Federal Reserve is adding securities to its securities portfolio at a fairly rapid pace.

And, Chairman Jerome Powell's term as the Fed chairman is to last only a few more weeks.

However, his term in office still has several years remaining.

Will he resign?

Will he create a space for Kevin Warsh to become the next Chairman?

This still has to be worked out.

The interesting thing to me in what the Federal Reserve has been doing these past twenty-one weeks.

I could make an argument that Mr. Powell and the Fed have created a monetary policy that is very open to Mr. Warsh becoming the Chairman of the Board of Governors and having a policy of quantitative easing already in place that would allow Warsh to step up the quantitative easing and get to two or three reductions to the Federal Funds rate this year.

Coming in now, Mr. Warsh will not have to "reverse" the direction of monetary policy.

Earlier in November, he would have had to "reverse" the direction in order to move from quantitative tightening to quantitative easing.

This "reversal" has already been completed.

Mr. Warsh will have a straight shot to lower rates.

We'll see.

The only thing I caution on has to do with the value of the U.S. dollar.

The value of the U.S. dollar has fallen since Mr. Trump took office in January 2025.

The general feeling is that the United States is heading for a period of rising inflation due to many of the policies the federal government has advanced over the past year.

Further reducing the Fed's policy rate of interest two or three more times this year may not be seen as healthy.

The value of the U.S. dollar may continue to drop...and could drop by a serious amount.

Inflation may be a major problem over the next several years.

This I am not in favor of.

data.title: REX American Resources Offers Potential, But Uncertainty Is A Problem
data.description:
One company that has really exceeded my expectations in recent months has been REX American Resources Corporation (REX). Conceptually, it is an interesting business. I say that because it focuses mostly on the production and sale of ethanol that is used for fuel. It does have some other things going forward as well. But that comprises most of the value of the business at this time. To be clear, the company does have certain catalysts that could result in additional value in the long run. But in my last article about it, published in December of last year, I made the decision to downgrade it from a Buy to a Hold.

This was because, even though the company had a lot going for it, including a very strong balance sheet, industry conditions remained challenged, leading to weakness in ethanol pricing. Long term, I was optimistic about export growth and lower corn input costs. But based on how the stock was priced, I could not in good conscience maintain my bullish stance. The good news is that REX stock had outperformed the market while I was bullish. Shares had risen 103.3% since I originally rated it a Buy in December of 2022. That dwarfed the 69.2% rise that the S&P 500 (SP500) saw.

But since downgrading it, the stock has risen even further. It's currently up 26.6%, which is well above the 3.8% rise that the S&P 500 saw. This is in spite of the fact that the company has historically seen a lot of volatility from operations and can be chalked up to optimism centered around some recent financial performance and expectations about what the future holds. However, I am not the kind of investor who is willing to write the check for a future that might fail to live up to expectations. Until I see concrete data that shows that the company will see a meaningful expansion in profitability, I think that being principled and maintaining my Hold rating is the right choice.

Digging Into REX American Resources

Purely from a fundamental standpoint, I would say that REX American Resources is doing fine, but not exceptional. The only new data that investors have access to since the time I last wrote about the business covers the final quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue amounted to $158 million. That was actually down from the $158.2 million that the business reported a year earlier. But even though revenue decreased, profitability skyrocketed. Net income surged from $11.1 million to $43.7 million. Operating cash flow more than doubled from $25.1 million to $53.8 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we get a surge from $19.6 million to $45.2 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business grew from $14.9 million to $21.5 million.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for 2025 in their entirety compared to 2024. Revenue increased some, but the real benefit came on the bottom line for the company. Profits and cash flows increased almost across the board. Only EBITDA worsened on a year-over-year basis. There is certainly a lot to unpack here in order to understand what is going on. As I mentioned at the start of this article, the company is an ethanol-based business. And historically, ethanol has experienced a lot of volatility in pricing. For the final quarter of 2025, for instance, the average sales price for ethanol rose to $1.72 compared to the $1.65 that it was a year earlier, even as the firm saw a decline in production from 74.6 million gallons to 70.1 million gallons. The end result here, unfortunately, was a drop in ethanol revenue from $122.8 million to $120.7 million.

This was not the only area of weakness for the business. Dried distillers grains revenue for the firm ended up falling from $23.9 million to $22.3 million. This was in spite of the fact that the average selling price actually increased from $143.80 per ton to $147.30 per ton. And it was the result of a drop in overall shipments amounting to 8.9%. On the other hand, the company did see a nice jump in distillers corn oil revenue from $10.4 million to $13.6 million. In addition to enjoying a rise in production from 23.5 million pounds to 25.1 million pounds, it saw the average sales price climb from $0.44 per pound to $0.54 per pound. This is interesting when you consider what is going on with the industry right now.

Earlier this month, USDA projected that about 91.3 million acres worth of corn will be harvested this year. That's up substantially from the 83 million that was harvested last year. In addition to acreage rising, there is the belief that yield per harvested acre will grow as well, climbing from 179.3 bushels per acre to 186.5 bushels. This translates to a 14.3% jump in overall production. And the belief is that the average price will fall from $4.24 per bushel to $4.15. I know that doesn't sound like much. But for the farming industry that has already been severely constrained in recent years, every little bit has an impact.

Even though lower pricing means lower revenue, all else being the same, it does not necessarily mean lower profits for a business such as REX American Resources. Around 80% of ethanol production costs involve corn. So as these input costs decline, there is the opportunity for margin expansion. Management specifically mentioned in their latest earnings call that reduced corn costs helped the company out. But obviously, the higher pricing that I detailed earlier also played a role here. In the chart below, you can see that, on a four-week average basis, U.S. exports of fuel ethanol continue to rise. This certainly bodes well for pricing even if overall ethanol production in the U.S. barely budges higher.

Another positive for the company, and perhaps the most significant one for the quarter, ended up being the 45Z tax credits that it received as a reduction to income tax expense. This is a fascinating program that was created under the Inflation Reduction Act and that extends from 2025 through 2029. Under this, different fuels get a carbon intensity score that measures total emissions. The lower this intensity score is, the higher the tax credit. And in some instances, when the carbon intensity score is high, a company might get no tax credit at all. Ethanol producers such as REX American Resources stand to benefit from this. Management actually believes that, at peak, it could get up to $150 million worth of tax benefits each year after the successful launch of its carbon sequestration assets. This is in addition to a credit of $85 per ton of carbon sequestered under the 45Q program that management believes could bring in additional tax credit benefits of around $36 million a year.

According to the company, during the final quarter of 2025, it recognized $28.1 million of tax credits under the 45Z program. This is significant in and of itself, and it was the main driver of profit growth during the quarter and the year. For 2026, there is the expectation that the business will continue to benefit from this program. However, there are other things that need to be taken into consideration. Most notably, we have its One Earth Energy ethanol production expansion and carbon capture projects. Management mentioned in their latest earnings release that they are nearing completion of the expansion of ethanol production at their One Earth facility, and they expect it to be fully operational sometime this year.

This is great, but there has been a bit of a setback when it comes to the company’s carbon capture and sequestration project. Even though management has done the work to get that project to fruition, the EPA timeline on approval has shifted. It was previously thought that it would be in June of this year. But now, that timeline has been moved to September. Because of this, for 2026 specifically, management does not expect any tax benefit associated with its carbon capture and sequestration activities. Only next year is that their hope that this will change.

Because of how volatile the picture is in this space, it's difficult to rely too much on financial performance data. And management sadly does not provide detailed guidance for investors. If we were to value the business based on results from the last two fiscal years, however, shares aren't exactly cheap. In fact, I would say that the business is somewhere between fairly valued and a bit overpriced. In the chart above, you can see exactly how the stock is valued at the moment. The EV to EBITDA multiple is especially unpleasant. But considering the opportunities that exist for the firm, I don't think that adopting a bearish outlook is necessary.

This is in part because, as the company completes the aforementioned projects and enjoys potentially large tax credits, it could see a nice boost in profitability. If we were to apply the maximum tax credits from both programs to the net income that the business generated in 2025, this would lower its price-to-earnings multiple for that year from 17.3 to just 6. But at this point, absent more concrete guidance from management, this is speculative and represents what I would consider to be the best-case scenario. Plus, it relies on a timeline that is highly uncertain and that wouldn’t yield this best-case scenario until at least next year. As a value investor, reliability is imperative. And if there is uncertainty that I am banking on, the discount already has to be baked in and clearly visible.

Takeaway

Based on all the data that exists today, I would argue that while I clearly downgraded the stock prematurely, the picture for the business does not justify upgrading it back to a Buy. The company does have certain catalysts that could push revenue and profitability higher this year and in future years. But because there is a lot of uncertainty over what that situation will look like, I am not ready to bank on it. Instead, using the data that we do have available, I believe that the firm is appropriately priced, and, as a result, I would argue that maintaining it as a Hold is prudent.
